allergens: gluten (chickpea flour may be processed in facilities with wheat)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of egg pakora consists of 4–5 pieces weighing approximately 200 g, commonly served as a snack or appetizer with chutney.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; USDA FoodData Central — Egg, whole, cooked, hard-boiled (FDC ID 173424) and Chickpea flour (besan) (FDC ID 175188)
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for egg pakora as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for Indian fried snacks and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for hard-boiled egg and chickpea flour (besan). The input values (energy 225 kcal, protein 10 g, fat 15 g, carbohydrate 12.5 g per 100 g) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin A, and B-vitamins are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid and micronutrient breakdown for this composite fried dish is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a single entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; chickpea flour-based product analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for egg pakora. GI estimated at 39 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for chickpea flour (besan)-based products (GI ~35–45, low category). The egg component contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (39 × 25.0) / 100 = 9.75, rounded to 9.8. Available carbohydrate per 200 g serving = 12.5 × 2 = 25.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100 g with limited potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Egg pakora contains 300 mg sodium per 100 g (600 mg per standard 200 g serving), approaching one-quarter of the 2,300 mg daily limit; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, reduce added salt in the batter, and avoid high-sodium dipping chutneys.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load with high fat from deep-frying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the chickpea flour batter has a low GI (~39) and the glycemic load per serving is moderate (~9.8), the high fat content (15 g/100 g) from deep-frying increases caloric density and may impair postprandial insulin sensitivity; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size and prefer air-fried or baked preparations.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High cholesterol (190 mg/100 g), saturated fat (3.5 g/100 g), trans fat (0.25 g/100 g), and total fat (15 g/100 g) from deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Egg pakora is high in dietary cholesterol (190 mg/100 g, 380 mg per serving) and contains saturated fat and trans fat from deep-frying; individuals with cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia should avoid or strictly limit consumption and consider baked or air-fried alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried preparation generates AGEs that exacerbate joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis; substituting with baking or air-frying is strongly recommended, and green chilli (a nightshade) should be monitored for individual tolerance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Egg Pakora (Hindi: अंडा पकोड़ा) is a popular Indian deep-fried snack consisting of hard-boiled eggs halved or quartered, coated in a spiced chickpea flour (besan) batter, and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The batter is typically seasoned with turmeric, red chilli powder, cumin, ginger, green chilli, and salt, giving the fritter a vibrant yellow-orange colour and a bold, savoury, mildly spicy flavour. The exterior is crunchy while the interior remains soft from the cooked egg. Egg pakora is widely consumed across India and in Indian diaspora communities in Singapore and Southeast Asia as a street food, tea-time snack, or appetizer, commonly served with mint chutney or tamarind chutney. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 225 kcal, 10 g protein, 15 g fat (including 3.5 g saturated fat and 0.25 g trans fat from frying), and 12.5 g carbohydrate with 2 g dietary fibre. Cholesterol is high at 190 mg/100 g due to the egg content. Sodium is 300 mg/100 g. The chickpea flour base gives a low glycemic index (~39), but the deep-frying process significantly increases caloric density and generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs), making this food a moderate-to-high concern for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. A standard serving is 4–5 pieces (approximately 200 g).
common_user_queries: How many calories in egg pakora?
Is egg pakora high in cholesterol?
Is egg pakora suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for egg pakora as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Indian fried snack data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for hard-boiled egg and chickpea flour (besan). The input values are internally consistent and plausible for this dish type. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for chickpea flour-based products. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation basis.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods and USDA FoodData Central (hard-boiled egg FDC ID 173424; chickpea flour FDC ID 175188); no single approved-source entry exists for egg pakora as a prepared composite dish. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, B-vitamin breakdown, and moisture data for egg pakora as a composite deep-fried dish are not available as a single entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI estimated at 39 (low) based on analogous chickpea flour-based product from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for egg pakora was found in approved sources. 4. The input ethnic_tags field contained 'Butter Chicken' which is inconsistent with egg pakora; this tag has been removed. country_of_origin corrected from 'Singapore' to 'India' as egg pakora is an Indian-origin dish, though it is widely consumed in Singapore. 5. Trans fat value of 0.25 g/100 g is retained from input; this is plausible for deep-fried products using commercial oils but should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis if available. 6. Cholesterol at 190 mg/100 g is consistent with the egg content (approximately one whole egg per 100 g of finished product after batter absorption).
